caveats: a: Occupation sources cover broader fabricated metal or manufacturing populations, not NAICS 332911.
a: Engineering-to-order and catalog assembly plants have materially different exposure profiles.
rho: No source directly observes five-year AI implementation in industrial-valve manufacturing.
rho: Nuclear, pipeline, and severe-service valves should implement more slowly than low-criticality waterworks products.
e: No public dataset separates contract manufacturing from proprietary product sales.
e: Configured-to-order products can resemble a service but remain ordinary product sales unless the manufacturer is executing an outsourced customer program.
s5: The owner-age proxy is not industry- or size-specific and measures owners rather than transactions.
s5: Corporate and sponsor-owned manufacturers have no individual-owner retirement trigger.
q: No public contract sample was available.
q: Capture is stronger for severe-service and highly qualified valves than for standard build-to-print products with multiple approved suppliers.
d5: The output series covers broader NAICS 33291 and ends in 2023.
d5: Pipeline capacity and water funding do not translate one-for-one into domestic LMM contract-valve revenue.
o: An accountable manufacturer remains necessary, but it may be a large integrated or offshore supplier rather than an LMM lens firm.
o: Labor automation is captured in a and rho and is not deducted again here.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring engineered or contract production billed per valve or program, often with material surcharges, testing and documentation, drawings, pressure class, metallurgy, actuation, trim, inspection, and delivery requirements.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS definition is broad product manufacturing rather than inherently outsourced service. The lens is narrowed to repeat build-to-print, private-label, configured-to-order, and customer-engineered production programs to avoid mixing service-like contract manufacturers with standard catalog sellers.
lens.excluded_activities: Standard catalog-product manufacturers without an outsourced production relationship, captive plants, fluid-power and plumbing-valve makers classified elsewhere, distributors, repair-only shops, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market industrial-valve manufacturers repeatedly providing build-to-print, private-label, configured-to-order, or customer-engineered valve production programs to external industrial, water, energy, and infrastructure customers.

a.bridge: BLS lists machinists, team assemblers, production supervisors, welders, and machine operators as common occupations in fabricated metal manufacturing, while its manufacturing overview reports production occupations are about half of manufacturing employment. I treat inquiry and specification review, quote and configuration work, drawing and bill-of-material analysis, production planning, procurement, quality documentation, test-record review, maintenance knowledge, and customer communication as AI-exposed, while keeping machining, welding, assembly, pressure testing, inspection, painting, handling, and accountable engineering release largely physical or human-controlled. The broad fabricated-metal mix may understate engineering content in custom valves or overstate it in repetitive assembly.
caveats: Occupation sources cover broader fabricated metal or manufacturing populations, not NAICS 332911.
Engineering-to-order and catalog assembly plants have materially different exposure profiles.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task studies from contract and engineered-valve manufacturers, mapped to current CAD, CPQ, ERP, machining, test, and quality-system automation, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Custom valve programs contain substantial technical and administrative work around configurations, materials, standards, quotes, drawings, tests, and documentation. Physical fabrication, assembly, inspection, and testing still comprise much of the wage-weighted task base.

d5.bridge: BLS real sectoral output for broader NAICS 33291 fell from an index of 100.360 in 2019 to 86.353 in 2023, while current-dollar output remained roughly $33.8 billion in 2022-23. Against this weakness, EIA reports 44.9 Bcf/day of planned US natural-gas pipeline capacity for 2026-27, 70% already under construction, and EPA identifies sustained water-infrastructure modernization and financing programs. These proxies cover all metal valves and downstream infrastructure rather than the eligible contract-manufacturing subset; project spending may favor large established suppliers and imports, so positive infrastructure signals can overstate LMM demand.
caveats: The output series covers broader NAICS 33291 and ends in 2023.
Pipeline capacity and water funding do not translate one-for-one into domestic LMM contract-valve revenue.
d5.change_evidence: Six-digit real shipments, customer-industry revenue cohorts, order backlogs, book-to-bill ratios, approved-vendor additions, municipal bid data, and five-year capital plans from water, LNG, pipeline, chemical, power, and industrial customers would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Industrial valves remain required in water, wastewater, pipelines, power, chemicals, and general process industries. Recent real-output decline tempers strong pipeline projects and public-water replacement needs, yielding a near-flat five-year base with wide project-cycle outcomes.

caveats: No public dataset separates contract manufacturing from proprietary product sales.
Configured-to-order products can resemble a service but remain ordinary product sales unless the manufacturer is executing an outsourced customer program.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm census of the injected LMM population using contract and private-label revenue, ownership of product IP, repeat-program share, customer specifications, captive status, and standalone operations would directly establish eligibility.
e.rationale: The industry is defined by manufacturing industrial and waterworks valves, not by providing outsourced production. Only firms whose core revenue is repeat build-to-print, private-label, configured-to-order, or customer-engineered manufacturing satisfy the fixed lens; standard proprietary catalog sales do not.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 332911 ($2,489,724,841) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 332911 ($14,220,422,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $434,517 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332911); class <5: 72 firms, avg revenue/firm $878,278 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $172,318 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 54 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,496,519 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $489,817 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 45 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,708,422 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $727,592 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 96 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,908,844 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,336,515 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 47 firms, avg revenue/firm $52,154,404 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,232,694 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 52 firms, avg revenue/firm $197,326,712 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $38,715,501 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 96

caveats: An accountable manufacturer remains necessary, but it may be a large integrated or offshore supplier rather than an LMM lens firm.
Labor automation is captured in a and rho and is not deducted again here.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers broadly replacing physical valves with software-only flow control, insourcing production, standardizing away custom programs, or deploying autonomous certified factories without an accountable manufacturer would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Remaining valve quantity requires accountable physical engineering, material procurement, machining or casting coordination, assembly, pressure and seat testing, marking, traceability, and release. Software can configure and document valves but cannot manufacture the pressure-containing product; limited demand may shift to standard catalog items, imports, or customer insourcing.

caveats: No public contract sample was available.
Capture is stronger for severe-service and highly qualified valves than for standard build-to-print products with multiple approved suppliers.
q.change_evidence: A representative set of supply agreements, quotes, and invoices showing open-book terms, metal surcharges, cost-down clauses, warranty allocation, qualification costs, annual reviews, win rates, and churn would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Engineering know-how, qualification, materials traceability, test records, approved drawings, reliability, and switching costs support retention. Competitive bids, material surcharges, framework agreements, annual sourcing reviews, customer cost-down requests, and alternative domestic or foreign suppliers share savings over five years.

caveats: No source directly observes five-year AI implementation in industrial-valve manufacturing.
Nuclear, pipeline, and severe-service valves should implement more slowly than low-criticality waterworks products.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments at engineered-valve manufacturers measuring quote engineering hours, configuration errors, schedule adherence, procurement labor, documentation time, nonconformance, test escapes, and accepted recommendations would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Five years supports AI use in CPQ, specification comparison, drawings and bills of material, scheduling, sourcing, test-record review, documentation, and maintenance. Product-liability risk, customer approvals, cybersecurity, CAD and ERP integration, materials traceability, pressure testing, and engineering sign-off constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding US employer-business owners were age 55 or older in 2018. I translate broad succession pressure into a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability and reduce it for corporate ownership, family transfers, internal reorganizations, closures, minority recapitalizations, and firms whose product approvals, IP, or customer dependence impair transfer. The all-industry owner population likely overstates individual-owner succession exposure in engineered industrial manufacturing.
caveats: The owner-age proxy is not industry- or size-specific and measures owners rather than transactions.
Corporate and sponsor-owned manufacturers have no individual-owner retirement trigger.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and succession interviews among independent valve manufacturers, plus five-year GF Data, Axial, PitchBook, and Capital IQ transactions coded for control, contract-manufacturing model, and standalone continuity, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Independent specialty valve manufacturers can face succession, but qualified product lines and customer approvals often attract strategic buyers or internal transfers and do not guarantee an LMM control sale.

principal_driver: Safety-critical physical infrastructure preserves demand and makes technical documentation and configuration workflows valuable.
principal_weakness: The industry predominantly sells proprietary products, so the recurring outsourced-service subset is small and unmeasured.

sources.supports: Defines industrial and municipal-water valve manufacturing and lists ball, butterfly, check, gate, globe, plug, solenoid, steam-trap, hydrant, and nuclear examples.
sources.title: NAICS definition: 332911 Industrial Valve Manufacturing

sources.supports: Explains that manufacturing transforms materials into new products and that contract processors generally remain classified in the manufacturing industry of the processed materials.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS manufacturing-sector definition

sources.supports: Lists machinists, team assemblers, production supervisors, welders, and machine operators among common fabricated-metal occupations and provides current earnings and hours.
sources.title: Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing: NAICS 332

sources.supports: Reports that production occupations represented about half of manufacturing employment in 2024 and describes continuing automation and maintenance needs.
sources.title: Producing the goods of the future: Job opportunities in manufacturing

sources.supports: Covers valve pressure-temperature ratings, dimensions, tolerances, materials, nondestructive examination, testing, and marking.
sources.title: ASME B16.34 Valves—Flanged, Threaded, and Welding End

sources.supports: Requires sound engineering design, material compatibility, hydrostatic shell and seat testing without leakage, position indication, and valve marking for hazardous-liquid pipelines.
sources.title: 49 CFR 195.116 Valves

sources.supports: Requires gas-pipeline valves to meet API 6D or equivalent performance, pressure-temperature limits, manufacturing tests, and anticipated operating conditions.
sources.title: 49 CFR 192.145 Valves

sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older in the 2019 ABS, data year 2018.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages: Over Half of U.S. Business Owners Were Age 55 and Over

sources.supports: Provides broader NAICS 33291 real-output index values, including 100.360 in 2019, 94.463 in 2022, and 86.353 in 2023.
sources.title: Real Sectoral Output for Metal Valve Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports broader NAICS 33291 current-dollar sectoral output of $33.931 billion in 2022 and $33.807 billion in 2023.
sources.title: Sectoral Output for Metal Valve Manufacturing

sources.title: Most planned natural gas pipeline capacity additions in 2026 and 2027 originate in Texas
